concepts.name: Performance-Based Scenario System
concepts.definition: A multi-phase system that links scenario planning to performance diagnosis, scenario exploration/development, implementation, assessment, and ongoing monitoring.

concepts.name: Predetermined Elements and Critical Uncertainties
concepts.definition: Predetermined elements are forces treated as relatively certain; critical uncertainties are high-impact, uncertain forces that structure the scenario matrix.

concepts.name: Results Assessment Domains
concepts.definition: Scenario project assessment should include satisfaction, learning, and performance rather than stopping with participant reactions.

arguments.claim: Scenario planning should be assessed as a performance intervention.
arguments.evidence: Chermack adapts results assessment to scenario projects, arguing that responsible performance improvement requires measuring satisfaction, learning, and system/financial outcomes where possible.

arguments.claim: Scenario development must move from forces to logics to implications.
arguments.evidence: The system ranks driving forces, separates predetermined elements from critical uncertainties, develops logics, writes plots, and constructs a research agenda.

cases.name: Performance diagnosis matrix
cases.context: Project preparation and assessment use performance categories such as capacity, outcomes, and stakeholder needs.
cases.illustrates: Scenario planning becomes rigorous when tied to specific performance questions.
cases.lesson: Define performance questions before writing stories.

logicChains.description: Project preparation → performance diagnosis → scenario exploration → scenario development → implementation/wind tunneling → assessment → monitoring.

terminology.definition: Testing a strategy, decision, or option against each scenario to see where it is robust or fragile.
A two-by-two structure built from two critical uncertainties.
Changes in participant knowledge, mental models, or strategic understanding.

nuance: Participant satisfaction matters but should not become the only measure.
Scenario outcomes are often indirect and delayed, so assessment must be realistic but not ignored.
